reasoning: Butterfly is a rigid property because an instance cannot cease to be a butterfly without losing its identity (it doesn't change into another kind of entity). It provides its own identity conditions for distinguishing one butterfly from another and defines a common unity criterion (it is one organism). It is not inherently dependent on the existence of another entity.

Color is anti-rigid (~R) because it can change over time or context, but 'Red' is Rigid (+R) because it is a specific property that cannot cease to be red without losing its identity. It carries identity conditions (+I) because if something is red, it cannot cease to be red without losing its identity. It does not supply its own identity conditions (-O) because it applies to diverse entities (e.g., an apple and a fire engine) that already carry their own identities. It is Non-Unifying (-U) because having one property (red) does not guarantee the presence of another (e.g., being a fruit). It is independent (-D) because the property of being red does not require the presence of another property to exist.

Food is Rigid because its members cannot cease to be food without losing their nature. It carries identity conditions (+I) based on its chemical composition. It does not supply its own identity conditions (-O) as it is a category that includes wildly different entities (e.g., fruits, meats, etc.). It is Unifying (+U) because it includes all consumable items. It is independent (+D) of other categories.

Animal is a rigid property because an instance cannot become an entirely different kind of entity without losing its identity (e.g., a cat cannot become a dog). It supplies its own identity conditions for distinguishing one animal from another and defines a common unity criterion (all animals share a set of characteristics). It is independent of other entities as it represents a distinct category of existence.

Rigidity (+R) because RedApple is a rigid property that cannot cease to be red or an apple without losing its identity. It carries (+I) and supplies its own identity conditions (+O). It is unifying (+U) because it defines a common unity criterion (e.g., red color and apple shape). It is independent (-D) because its existence does not depend on the existence of other entities.

A Group is rigid because its members cannot cease to be a group and remain the same type of entity. It supplies its own identity conditions (+O) based on membership criteria. It is unifying (+U) because it defines a single entity (the group) composed of distinct parts (members). It is independent (-D) as it represents a well-defined category of entities.

A country is typically rigid because once a piece of land is internationally recognized as a sovereign state, it remains so. It supplies its own identity conditions (e.g., a defined territory) and is characterized by unity (it is one entity). It is not inherently dependent on other entities.

According to Guarino and Welty (2000), AmountOfMatter is Rigid (+R) because if something is an amount of matter, it cannot cease to be so without losing its identity. It carries identity conditions (+I) inherited from its parent category 'Physical Entity'. It does not supply its own identity conditions (-O) as it is a property that applies to an entity (a physical object) rather than the entity itself being the property. It is Non-Unifying (-U) because it cannot distinguish individual instances of the same type (e.g., two lumps of the same matter). It is independent (+D) of the specific physical boundaries or the spatial distribution of matter.

PhysicalObject is a rigid sortal because an instance cannot become another kind of thing without losing its identity (e.g., a piece of wood cannot cease to be a physical object without ceasing to exist). It carries an identity condition (+I) based on its internal boundaries (+O) and does not supply its own identity conditions (it is not anti-unity). It is non-dependent (+D) because it represents a common property of many different entities.

A GroupOfPeople is typically rigid because once a collection is identified as a group, it remains one. It supplies its own identity conditions (who is included) and is characterized by unity (it is a single entity). It is not inherently dependent on other types of entities.

LivingBeing is rigid because an entity cannot cease to be a living being without ceasing to exist. It supplies its own identity conditions (global identification of an individual organism) and is characterized by unity (constituting a single organic whole). It is not inherently dependent on the existence of other entities.

SocialEntity is Rigid (+R) because if an entity is social, it is essentially the same social entity throughout its existence. It carries identity conditions (+I) based on its unique combination of social characteristics (e.g., a group of people). It does not supply its own identity conditions (-O) because it is a high-level generalization that includes wildly different individuals (e.g., organizations and legal departments) that do not share a common identity criterion. It is Non-Unifying (-U) as it does not guarantee a whole part relationship, and it is Independent (-D) as it represents a distinct category of existence.

Location is typically defined by rigid boundaries (+R) and carries an identity condition (+I) based on spatial reference systems. It usually does not supply its own identity conditions (-O) as it is often relative (e.g., a region) or depends on a reference system. It is non-unifying (~U) because it cannot be the only entity of its kind and is independent (-D) as it represents a space or position.

Fruit is Rigid because if an instance is a fruit, it cannot cease to be a fruit without losing its identity. It carries an Identity Condition (+I) based on its internal structure and composition. It does not supply its own Identity Condition (-O) because it is a category that includes different types (e.g., apple, banana) with their own identities. Fruit is Unifying (+U) because it includes all the different types of fruit, and it is independent (+D) of other categories.

Vertebrate is a rigid property because an organism cannot cease to be a vertebrate without ceasing to exist. It supplies its own identity conditions (IC) for an organism, such as the presence of a notochord or a vertebral column. It is unifying because an organism cannot be both a vertebrate and a non-vertebrate simultaneously.

A caterpillar is a rigid entity because it represents a specific organic entity (an insect larva) with a constant identity throughout its growth process. It supplies its own identity conditions (IC) for distinguishing individual caterpillars and defines a common unity criterion (biological wholeness). It is not inherently dependent on the existence of other entities.

A Person is typically rigid because once an entity is identified as a person, it cannot cease to be a person without losing its identity (e.g., a legal or social identity). It carries an identity condition (+I) based on its unique combination of properties such as a name, date of birth, and distinguishing characteristics. It usually does not supply its own identity conditions (-O) because they are defined by external agents or legal systems. It is non-unifying (~U) because it cannot be divided into smaller parts that remain persons. It is independent (-D) because a person's existence does not depend on the existence of another entity.

LegalAgent is typically rigid because once an entity is identified as a legal agent, it remains one. It supplies its own identity conditions (e.g., a corporation or an individual) and is characterized by unity (it is a single entity with defined responsibilities). It is independent of other types of entities.

The term 'Entity' is Rigid because it is a well-defined concept (+R) that does not supply its own Identity Condition (-O) or carry Unity (-U) or Dependence (+D) because it is the root of all existence.

An organization is typically rigid because once it is established, it remains the same entity (+R). It carries an identity condition (+I) based on its unique constitution (articles of incorporation, etc.) and does not supply its own identity conditions (-O). It is non-unifying (~U) because it can be composed of diverse departments or subsidiaries that do not share a common unity criterion. It is independent (-D) as it is not inherently dependent on other entities to exist.

Agent is a rigid property because an entity cannot become an agent without ceasing to be the same entity. It supplies its own identity conditions (IC) for distinguishing one agent from another and defines a common unity criterion (e.g., intentional behavior).

Apple is a rigid property because an instance of an apple cannot cease to be an apple without losing its identity (it doesn't change into another kind of thing). It provides its own identity conditions for distinguishing one apple from another and defines a unity criterion (all parts are internal to the whole). It is not inherently dependent on the existence of other entities.
